---
title: "Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision"
chapter_id: "claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "spinoza"
  - "viea"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "aletheia"
  - "uat"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `spinoza`, `viea`, `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `spinoza`, `viea`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `spinoza`, `viea`, `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat`; raw cache: `spinoza`, `viea`, `aletheia`, `uat`; connector/recovery: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

The architecture needs a durable epistemic layer for claims, evidence, contradictions, uncertainty, and revision.

The VCM chapters decide what context is admitted and adequate. Reasoning now needs to decide what the system believes, why it believes it, how strongly it can say it, and what happens when evidence changes. Spinoza supplies the core discipline: neural systems can propose claims, but claims need structured support states, provenance, contradiction links, defeaters, and downgrade paths before they become commitments.

In the book itself, Appendix C is the public claim ledger. The architecture should generalize that discipline: prose is an output view, not the place where belief state lives.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model can generate plausible prose without maintaining why it believes a claim or how new evidence changes prior beliefs.

A fluent answer can contain assumptions that were never extracted, contradictions that were never linked, and claims that keep their confident tone after evidence weakens. Belief inertia is the epistemic version of technical debt: a claim remains in circulation because no artifact forces it to be revised.

Aletheia and UAT both push against this failure by decomposing outputs into propositions, preserving unsupported or inferred states, and routing uncertainty to review. The claim ledger gives those states a durable home.

## Core Claim

[claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Reasoning should maintain claim ledgers with support states, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, and revision history.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture, Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture, The Coherence Exchange, Aletheia Foundry, and Unified Adaptive Tribunal - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Belief Revision Record.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Generated or extracted claim"] --> B["Claim record"]
  B --> C["Evidence refs + support state"]
  C --> D["Contradiction / defeater scan"]
  D --> E{"Revision needed?"}
  E -- "no" --> F["Retain with state"]
  E -- "yes" --> G["Revise / downgrade / block"]
  G --> H["Revision history"]
  F --> H
  H --> I["Claim ledger"]
```

- Extract claims into structured records.
- Attach evidence tier, provenance, and uncertainty.
- Revise beliefs when contradictions or stronger evidence appear.

The ledger separates claim text, claim label, support state, evidence refs, uncertainty, contradictions, revision reason, and review status. A claim can remain useful while unsupported, speculative, contradicted, or blocked, but it must be labeled accordingly.

Belief revision is not just promotion. It includes downgrade, deprecation, split, merge, contradiction preservation, and residual creation. Negative evidence is an asset because it prevents the system from relearning the same false confidence.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Belief Revision Record.

- VCM supplies source context.
- Spinoza verifies claims.
- Evidence matrix records book-level claims.

Minimum fields:

- `revision_id`
- `claim_id`
- `prior_support_state`
- `new_support_state`
- `evidence_refs`
- `contradiction_refs`
- `defeaters`
- `revision_reason`
- `uncertainty`
- `review_status`
- `history_refs`

VCM supplies source context. Spinoza verifies or downgrades claims. UAT can review contested claims. Appendix C records book-level claim support while preserving revision history.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Claims are separable from prose.
- Contradictions remain linked.
- Revision history is not overwritten.

The non-overwrite invariant is central. New evidence can change the current state, but it cannot erase the fact that a prior state existed, which evidence supported it, and why it changed.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Untracked assumptions.
- Belief inertia.
- Contradiction deletion.

Untracked assumptions should become claims or residuals. Belief inertia should trigger review when contradictions or stronger evidence arrive. Contradiction deletion is unacceptable: contradictions can be resolved, bounded, or refuted, but they should remain linked.

## Minimal implementation

A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example.

The smallest useful implementation is the existing `claim_record` schema plus a `belief_revision_record` schema and fixture. The fixture should demonstrate conservative support-state handling without claiming that an actual contested claim has been resolved.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Claim extraction test | Check that prose claims can be represented separately from prose with labels, support states, and provenance. | planned; not run |
| Contradiction detection test | Check that contradiction refs remain linked instead of being overwritten. | planned; not run |
| Belief revision test | Check that support-state updates preserve prior evidence and revision history. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:claims.ledger.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A claim update preserves prior evidence and revision history. | planned |
| `lean:claims.ledger.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A contradiction prevents promotion until resolved, bounded, or recorded as residual. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `aletheia` | Aletheia Foundry | safe_general_intelligence_lineage | Safe general intelligence predecessor. Use ideas; avoid final branding if collision remains. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `uat` | Unified Adaptive Tribunal | evaluation_refinement | Multi-AI collaborative refinement/evaluation protocol. | source note available; local raw cache available |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision makes belief state durable. Claims are separated from prose, tied to evidence and uncertainty, linked to contradictions, and revised without overwriting history.

The chapter's rule for the living book is also the rule for the stack: a claim can be written beautifully and still remain unsupported until the ledger says otherwise.
